specification.topic-statement: The control socket system accepts JSON commands from external clients over a Unix domain socket and dispatches responses, including deferred responses for the wait_idle command.

scope.in-scope: Server initialization, connection lifecycle, message dispatch, all four command handlers, the deferred wait_idle mechanism, and connection teardown.
scope.boundary: The read_framebuffer command delegates serialization of the rendered terminal state to a separate subsystem. This specification records what is passed to that subsystem and what is returned — the serialization logic itself is out of scope.
The read_token_cache command reads from the token accounting subsystem. This specification records what values are read and how they are assembled — the token cache internals are out of scope.
The send_keys command appends decoded bytes to a key injection buffer. This specification records when and how that append occurs — the downstream processing of injected keys is out of scope.
The wait_idle command observes the agent's current state. This specification records what value is observed — the state machine transitions are out of scope.

field.name: keys
field.note: send_keys only; the key sequence to inject

field.name: timeout_ms
field.note: wait_idle only; milliseconds before timeout response

field.name: type
field.note: present on success responses; value is command-specific

field.name: error
field.note: present on error responses; human-readable description

field.name: turns
field.note: each object has index (integer) and tokens (integer)

field.name: client file descriptor
field.note: negative one when no client is connected

field.name: wait idle pending
field.note: true when a wait_idle response has been deferred

field.name: wait idle deadline
field.note: monotonic clock milliseconds at which a deferred wait_idle times out

behavior.server-initialization:
The server is initialized with a runtime directory path and produces a running socket server.
The runtime directory is created if it does not exist, with permissions granting access only to the owner (mode 0700). If the directory already exists, no error is produced. If creation fails, initialization fails and no socket is created.
A Unix domain stream socket is created. The socket path is constructed by combining the runtime directory with the current process identifier in the form `ikigai-.sock`.
If the path would exceed the platform's maximum socket path length (108 bytes on Linux), initialization fails.
Any file already at the socket path is removed before binding. The socket is bound to that path and begins listening with a backlog of one pending connection.
If initialization fails at any step, any socket file descriptor created during the attempt is closed. The caller receives an error. The runtime treats the control socket as unavailable and continues operating without it.
behavior.event-loop-integration:
The server integrates with the application's main I/O multiplexing loop. On each iteration, the server contributes its file descriptors to the set of descriptors to monitor.
The listening socket is included in the monitored set unless a wait_idle response is currently deferred. When a deferred wait_idle is pending, the listening socket is excluded, preventing new connections from being accepted until the deferred response is sent.
A client connected and waiting for a wait_idle response holds exclusive access to the server until that response fires. The backlog of one means at most one additional connection attempt will queue; any further attempts are refused by the operating system.
When a client is connected, its file descriptor is always included in the monitored set, regardless of deferred state.
On each loop iteration, processing occurs in this order: first, the deferred wait_idle tick runs; then, a pending new connection is accepted if the listening socket is ready; then, a pending client request is handled if the client socket is ready.

connection-lifecycle.accepting-connections:
When the listening socket becomes ready, a connection is accepted. If a client is already connected when a new connection arrives, the existing client's file descriptor is closed without notification before the new connection is accepted.
Clients have no protection against eviction. A new incoming connection will silently terminate any currently connected client, including one waiting for a deferred wait_idle response.
connection-lifecycle.request-handling:
When the connected client's socket becomes ready, up to 4095 bytes are read. If the read returns zero bytes (EOF) or a negative value (error), the client is disconnected: its file descriptor is closed, the client file descriptor is set to negative one, and any pending wait_idle state is reset.
If bytes are read successfully, the data is null-terminated. A newline character, if present, is replaced with a null terminator, discarding everything from the newline onward.
If a request arrives without a trailing newline, it is processed as received. There is no waiting for a complete message before dispatch.
connection-lifecycle.sending-responses:
Responses are sent using the MSG_NOSIGNAL flag to prevent SIGPIPE signals on broken pipe connections.
If the send fails or is partial, the client file descriptor is closed immediately, any pending wait_idle state is reset, and the failure is not reported to the caller.
After a response is sent successfully (for all commands except the deferred case), the client connection is closed.
The protocol is strictly one-request, one-response, one-close. Clients must reconnect to send another request.

behavior.message-dispatch:
The received bytes are parsed as JSON. If parsing fails, an error response is sent immediately: `{"error":"Invalid JSON"}`.
The `type` field of the parsed JSON object determines the command. The following type values are recognized: `read_framebuffer`, `read_token_cache`, `send_keys`, and `wait_idle`. Any other value, or the absence of the `type` field, produces an error response: `{"error":"Unknown message type"}`.

commands.read-framebuffer:
The current terminal framebuffer is serialized and returned.
If no framebuffer is available, the response is: `{"error":"No framebuffer available"}`.
Otherwise, the framebuffer bytes, terminal dimensions (rows and columns), cursor position (row and column), and input buffer visibility flag are passed to the framebuffer serialization subsystem. The serialized result is returned as the response.
If serialization fails, the response is: `{"error":"Serialization failed"}`.
commands.read-token-cache:
The current agent's token accounting data is read and returned as a JSON object.
If no agent is active or no token cache is available, the response is: `{"total_tokens":0,"budget":100000,"turn_count":0,"context_start_index":0,"turns":[]}`.
Otherwise, the total token count, budget, turn count, and context start turn index are read from the cache. A JSON array is constructed containing one object per turn, each with an `index` field (the context start index plus the turn's zero-based position) and a `tokens` field. The response assembles all of these into a JSON object.
The turns array grows with the number of turns. No limit is imposed on the size of the response.
commands.send-keys:
A sequence of key characters is injected into the application's input.
The `keys` field is extracted from the request. If absent, the response is `{"error":"Missing keys field"}`.
The keys string is unescaped to produce a raw byte sequence. If unescaping fails, the response is `{"error":"Failed to unescape keys"}`.
On success, the response `{"type":"ok"}` is sent and the connection is closed. The unescaped byte sequence is then appended to the key injection buffer for processing on the next event loop iteration.
The client receives the success response and the connection is closed before the keys are submitted to the input pipeline. The effect of the keys is not visible until a subsequent event loop iteration.
When the C client library constructs the send_keys JSON request, it embeds the keys string directly into the JSON without any escaping. A keys string containing double-quote characters, backslashes, or newlines will produce malformed JSON, which the server will reject with an invalid JSON error.
commands.wait-idle:
The command waits until the current agent enters the idle state, up to a specified timeout.
If a wait_idle response is already deferred from a previous request, the response is `{"error":"wait_idle already pending"}` and the command is not processed further.
The `timeout_ms` field is extracted. If absent or not a positive integer, the response is `{"error":"Missing or invalid timeout_ms"}`.
If the agent's current state is idle at the moment the request is processed, the response is `{"type":"idle"}` and the connection is closed normally.
If the agent is not idle, the response is deferred. The current monotonic clock time is read and the deadline is set to that time plus the requested timeout in milliseconds. The wait_idle pending flag is set. No response is sent and the connection is kept open.
On each subsequent event loop iteration, the deferred tick checks two conditions:
First, if the agent's state is now idle, the response `{"type":"idle"}` is sent and the connection is closed.
Second, if the monotonic clock time has reached or passed the deadline, the response `{"type":"timeout"}` is sent and the connection is closed.
In both cases, the wait_idle pending flag is cleared.
The timeout is checked once per event loop iteration, not on a dedicated timer. The actual elapsed time before a timeout response is sent may exceed the requested timeout by up to one full event loop cycle.
If the deferred response send fails (e.g., because the client disconnected), the connection is closed and the wait_idle pending flag is cleared silently. No error is surfaced.

behavior.server-shutdown: During shutdown, the client connection (if any) is closed, the listening socket is closed, and the socket file is removed from the filesystem.
